INTRODUCTION TO YOUTUBE
Of all the ways to generate paid traffic, there is nothing that touches the potential of
Google Ads.
The first thing to wrap your head around is the vast scope of Google Ads. Search is only
the beginning. As you probably know, Google owns YouTube, who is the dominant
player in video ads. What you may not know is that Google also gives you the ability to
advertise on millions of different websites in their advertising network.
Grant Cardone and our team here at 10X spend millions every year on Google Ads.
More importantly, we make many millions more back. In this manual, and the
supporting videos and documents, you’re going to learn all forms of Google
advertising. We’re going to take you from setup to launching, to optimizing, to scaling.
As a Google advertiser, you literally have unlimited opportunity for growth. Not only
does Google give you the ability to reach more customers than any other network, it is
also the fastest way on earth to get in front of them. You can literally be running ads
and making sales in a matter of minutes.
Our goal is to help you start getting results immediately. Let’s go!
While it can take years to master Google and Youtube ads,
you don’t need to be a master to make real money. Just
create an ad, start a campaign and get in the game.
GOOGLE SEARCHES
8.5+ Billion Daily Searches
YOUTUBE VIDEOS
60+ Billion Daily Minutes
GOOGLE NETWORK
25+ Billion Daily Displays
Be sure to check out the step-by-step Google Ads Action Plan!
10xdownload.com/ga2

GOOGLE ADS GLOSSARY
Everywhere I go people tell me that they see my ads
everywhere. It’s because I’m not afraid to spend money
to invest in my long-term success. Go big or go bigger.
Ad setting which allows you to control how Google uses your budget each day,
including the time and speed of delivery.
AD DELIVERY
A feature of search ads, extensions allow a business to add additional information to an
ad such as business info or additional links
AD EXTENSIONS
The part of an ad campaign that defines your targeting by keywords, demographics,
interests, location, and more.
AD GROUP
How Google prioritizes the ads it displays, which is determined by your bid and
quality score.
AD RANK
A tool that automatically updates your campaigns, ad groups, or keywords based on
conditions you set.
AUTOMATED RULES
A keyword match type that permits your ad to be shown whenever someone searches
that phrase, or a phrase that is similar to it.
BROAD MATCH
The text, video or other content that is displayed to a Google user across any of their
properties or network affiliates.
AD
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
6
While it may seem like a lot, marketing online isn’t rocket
science. I’m proof of that. Learn the basics, and understand
the steps to take someone from an ad viewer to a buyer.
Highest level of the ad hierarchy, the campaign includes budget, location, targeting,
language and other many other settings.
CAMPAIGN
Engagement with your ad and the page it links to. Every time a user is served your ad
and clicks on it, a click is logged.
CLICK
The percentage of people that clicked on an ad after it was served to them.
CLICK THROUGH RATE (CTR)
A key action taken on your website such as an opt-in, donations, purchase, etc.
CONVERSION
Google Ads tool which helps track and measure the valuable actions your customers
make after clicking on your Google Ads ad. Relevant conversions include sign-ups,
purchases, etc.
CONVERSION TRACKING
The amount you actually paid for a click on your ad, regardless of how your bidding
strategy.
COST PER CLICK (CPC)
The cost an advertiser is paying every time their advertisements receives 1,000
impressions.
COST-PER-THOUSAND IMPRESSIONS (CPM)
The average cost to an advertiser every time someone views one of their video ads.
COST-PER-VIEW (CPV)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
7
Google Ads is one of these things you’ve got to be mentally
prepared for. It isn’t easy, and it won’t happen overnight.
That said, when you crack the code, you can 10X very fast.
A keyword match type that tells Google to only show the ad affiliated with the keyword
if a user types that phrase in exactly.
EXACT MATCH
The average number of times a unique user sees your ad over a given period.
FREQUENCY
The setting that allows you to choose in which geographic area your ads are shown to.
GEOTARGETING
Global Site Tag is one of the conversion codes you need to include in your website(s)
when setting up conversion tracking.
GLOBAL SITE TAG
Google’s free web analytics tool that allows you to see exactly what users are doing on
your website.
GOOGLE ANALYTICS
Customer ID is a unique number associated with a user's Google Ads account. It’s a
3-part number which can be shown in the top right corner of Google Ads dashboard.
CUSTOMER ID
The maximum amount you’re willing to spend on a campaign in a given day.
DAILY BUDGET
The URL of the page on your site that you send users to after they click on your ad.
DESTINATION URL
The URL that is actually shown to users in your ad. This doesn’t need to exactly match
the destination URL, as long as you aren’t baiting-and-switching users.
DISPLAY URL
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
8
You’ve got to constantly be studying other people’s ads.
Every Google search, every time you watch a YouTube video,
every time you shop. Watch who spends the big money.
The large network of websites that run Google's ads.
GOOGLE DISPLAY NETWORK
An impression is counted any time your ad appears in front of a user. This could be on
the results page of Google search, a video thumbnail on YouTube, or any other type of ad.
IMPRESSION
An interaction is when a user performs an advertiser-defined action that is valuable to
their business. These actions include clicks on ads, views on videos, and more.
INTERACTIONS
Words or phrases that you choose to associate with your ads. For example, when a user
searches for something on Google that matches to a keyword in your account, the
affiliated ad will appear in the search results.
KEYWORD
A tool in the Google Ads interface used for finding new keywords, estimating search
volume, and other competition metrics.
KEYWORD PLANNER
An indicator of the current activity of a campaign, ad group, or individual ad. Each level
can be approved, disapproved, paused, or active.
STATUS
Allows advertisers to select websites, videos, and mobile apps of their interest
specifically on Google Display Network.
MANAGED PLACEMENTS
The maximum amount you’re willing to pay for a click.
MAX CPC
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
9
Think about your ads not just as a source of revenue, but
also as market research. When you create new products,
don’t just guess what people want. Run ads and find out.
The various locations on Google Display Network where advertisers ads are shown to
users.
PLACEMENTS
Where an ad appears on the search engine results page (SERP) your ad was shown.
POSITION
A score from 1-10 that Google assigns to indicate the quality pertaining to the keyword,
ad and landing page. The closer to 10, the better!
QUALITY SCORE
Google Ads feature which allows advertisers to reach customers who have visited their
website. Also referred to as retargeting.
REMARKETING
Indicator of whether campaigns are profitable or not. ROAS is the average value
advertisers make for every dollar they spend on their ads.
RETURN ON AD SPEND (ROAS)
A keyword match type that excludes your ad from being served for certain search
terms.
NEGATIVE KEYWORDS
Results on the page of a search engine results page that are not paid for by an
advertiser.
ORGANIC SEARCH RESULTS
A keyword match type that permits your ad to be shown only when a specific phrase is
included somewhere in a search.
PHRASE MATCH

Get your Google Ads and our Facebook Ads working
together. They say the average consumer takes 7 exposures
to do business with you, so use every channel possible.
Way to adjust Google Ads and Google Analytics reports (tables & charts) to see data as
per advertiser's requirement.
SEGMENT
The number of people who clicked a link. If the same person clicks the same link twice,
it only counts as one unique click.
UNIQUE CLICK
Word or multiple word phrase that users type in the Google search bar. Search terms
trigger advertisers keywords and corresponding ads.
SEARCH TERM (SEARCH QUERY)
Google page where the results of a search query are displayed.
SEARCH ENGINE RESULTS PAGE (SERP)

GOOGLE ADS OVERVIEW
As a new advertiser in Google, you will manage every aspect of advertising from your
Google Ads account. While we will cover the ad manager in great detail in an upcoming
chapter, for now you just need to get it set up.
Smart Mode - Simplified version that takes away a lot of options and forces you to set
up your first ad immediately.
Expert Mode - Full-featured version that gives you all the options and full control.
As you can see below, you’ve got a few types of accounts you can create. If you’re
driving traffic online to websites or funnels, you’ll likely want to choose that option…
AD ACCOUNT AND SETUP
AD MANAGER AND SETUP
Start your account setup at 10xresource.com/ga4
You can always change your mode later in the Tools section.
IMPORTANT NOTE: Google now has 2 modes for ad accounts to choose from.

Which do we recommend?
If you want to do the bare minimum on Google Ads and simply get them going,
then go with Smart Account (which is the default setup). If you want to get
serious about Google Ads and continue learning and growing, then choose the
Expert Account. In this manual, we’ll assume you chose the Expert option.
Set up your billing.
For your first ad to go live, you’ll need a credit card
on file. Depending on what setup you choose, you
may do this during setup. If not, simply go to
Tools → Billing → Summary
While you don’t need any other accounts to get your ads running, there are a few
you’ll likely want to create and connect. All account connections can be established
by going to...
YouTube Channel
You’ll need to have a YouTube channel set up if you want to run
video ads. You can use your same Google account (email) or a
different one. Go to YouTube.com and when logged in, click on the
icon and create a channel.
Google Merchant Center
If you sell products online via an ecommerce shop, then you will
definitely want to set this up. The Google Merchant Center allows
you to feed your products to Google Shopping and to display them
in various types of ads, which we will discuss.
Google Analytics
While it isn’t mandatory, if you want to take your tracking to the
next level, you’ll want to create an Analytics account. We’ll cover this
more in a later chapter.
ADDITIONAL ACCOUNTS
Linked Accounts	Tools 	Setup

15
HOW YOU’RE CHARGED
First and foremost, understand that Google Ads pricing is entirely driven by supply
and demand. There is ad inventory, which includes ads across the entire Google
network, and there are advertisers competing for that space. The entire system is
basically a big auction.
Since there are so many ad types on Google, there are also a variety of ways that you
will be charged. With that said, if you understand the basics, you’ll be able to spend
more confidently. Generally speaking, you’re either paying for...
As you’ll learn in the upcoming chapter on bidding, much of this process is now
automated by Google. That said, you need to fundamentally understand how money
flows to ensure you’re making smart decisions.
IMPORTANT NOTE: In both scenarios, a key statistic is Cost Per Click (CPC). In
the case of PPC ads, this is what you’re paying for the click based on the
auction. For exposure-based ads, the CPC is calculated by dividing the cost you
pay for exposures by how many people click.
This is an oversimplified example, but the basic premise holds true.
CLICKS - Cost incurred only if user clicks.
All ads referred to as Pay Per Click (PPC).
Includes search ads, shopping ads, local ads, and more.
EXPOSURES - Cost incurred from views, whether users clicks or not.
Text or image ads referred to as Cost Per Thousand (CPM).
Video ads referred to as Pay Per View (PPV).
- or -

16
Remember that you are playing in Google’s sandbox. Always
run your ads with integrity and provide a good experience.
Never run negative ads or try to game the system.
GETTING ADS DISPLAYED
While your willingness to spend more than your competitors is certainly a driver in
your traffic, it’s not actually the entire story. Let’s look at the simple formula that
determines the position of your ads, or what is known as Adrank...
While there are lots of stats and metrics that make up the advertising life cycle, at the
end of the day it’s all about the bottom line. If you keep an eye on thes 3 stats and
focus all of your energy on optimizing for them, you’ll be in good shape...
Adrank = Bid + Quality Score
In addition to helping determine where your ads are displayed, your Quality
Score also helps determine the cost of your ad. In order to provide users with the
best possible experience, Google rewards you for providing a quality experience.
Here are the factors that affect your Quality Score:
Clickthrough rate - The percentage of people who click your ad when it is displayed.
Keyword relevance - Includes the keywords in the ad settings and/or the ad itself.
Landing page - Your page should perform well (speed) and be relevant to the ad.
Complaints - Google allows users to report ads, greatly damaging your score.
Cost Per Conversion
How much you pay for any conversion, including leads.
Total Ad Spend / Number of Conversions = Cost Per Conversion
You want your Cost/Conv to be as low as possible.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
17
You’ve got to be willing to pay to play. You’ll never crack the
code on day one, so make sure you set aside an initial ad
budget that you’re willing to invest as you learn.
If you’re like me, you hate complicated stuff like the Ads
Manager. The good news is, you don’t need 80% of the features.
Focus on the most important functions, and you’ll be just fine.
Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)
The amount of money it costs to get a new customer.
Total Ad Spend / Number of Customers = CPA
You want your CPA to be as low as possible.
Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)
The percentage return (sales) on your advertising spend.
(Return on Ads - Ad Spend) / Ad Spend = ROAS
You want your ROAS to be as high as possible.
AD MANAGER BASICS
By now, you may have already logged into the Ad Manager. If not, you can always
access it by going to ads.google.com.
The Ads Manager is a very powerful tool that can take a long time to fully understand.
While we will provide some basics, the best way to learn is to watch the tutorial videos
and just start playing with it.

AD CAMPAIGN STRUCTURE
When you get into the Ad Manager, you’ll see a dashboard that will provide some
great insights. In addition to the most important stats, you’ll be able to get a snapshot
of your best performing ads, basic demographics, and any changes to be aware of.
We will talk more about how to find valuable data, but if you understand these basics,
you can get in the game. It is also important to realize that there is work to do in order
to start receiving data. We will discuss conversion tracking in the next chapter.
Of everything you need to know to run ads, this is the most important as it is how ads
are actually created. While every type of ad has a different setup process, there are a
lot of similarities in each.
With all that said, before diving in, you do want to understand the basic structure of
how ads are organized. There are 3 levels to understand that generally work in this
hierarchy…
The good news is that running ads on Google is a step-by-step process.
Click the New Campaign button and you’re off to the races!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Ad Group A 	Ad Group B
Campaign 1
Ad 1 	Ad 2 	Ad 3 	Ad 4
Campaign - Highest level where you
choose your objective, ad type, goal,
budget and settings.
Ad group - Second level where you define
your audience, include keywords and all
targeting.
Ad - This is the actual ad “creative” which
could include text ads, video ads, or
product ads.
We will discuss each aspect of your
campaigns more in future chapters.
PRO TIP
If you want more control over your ads, you can create a new campaign for every
ad group, rather than having multiple ad groups under one campaign. This will
allow you to manually edit your budget for every audience you target.
TIPS FOR NAVIGATION
Getting around the ad manager can take a bit of getting used to. These tips will help…
Options at top of menu before and after clicking on a campaign
The sidebar menu is your main navigation for the day-to-day management of ads. While
the labels are relatively clear (if you know the terminology), it helps to understand that
the menu options displayed change depending on what page you are on.
TIP #1 	Using the Sidebar Menu

As you build ads, you will click through the steps, which can differ for each type of ad.
As we mentioned, there is a standardized hierarchy. To avoid confusion, and to easily go
back and forth between steps, you’ll want to use the campaign navigation in the top left.
While this one may be fairly obvious, it is helpful to know that when you change the
date range, it will remain set for those dates on all pages until it is changed. As you can
see, you can customize to any date range, or choose a number of preset options.
We use very detailed campaign names with abbreviations for easy reference.
SKAG is our code for “single keyword ad group”. You can make up your own!
TIP #2 	Using Campaign Navigation
TIP #3 	Changing the Date Range
After you’ve created ads, you’ll likely want to go back to make edits. That can be done on
the Campaign, Ad Group or Ad level. In order to view settings or make changes, you’ll
want to click the “More Details” link at the very top of the page. This can trick some
people up.
TIP #4 	Click More Details to Make Edits

As you run more and more campaigns, your Ad Manager will begin to fill up. Some ads
will be active, some will be paused, and some will be removed. To make it easier to only
view the Campaigns, Ad Groups, and Ads you want, there is a filter feature above the
columns. While there are many filter options, the most common and important is the
Status filter.
Use this to get a clear picture. Also, realize that if you don’t see a Campaign, Ad Group or
Ad that you’re looking for, it may be filtered out. Simply click to adjust.
TIP #5 	Using Campaign Status Filter
Having the right data in front of you is critical, and to do so you’ll need to customize your
columns on your Campaign, Ad Group, and Ad views. Google allows you to customize the
column data you can view. We are going to give you much more details on this in the
Data and Analytics chapter, but it is worth noting again because it is so important.
TIP #6 	Column Customization is Critical

GETTING STARTED
© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
24
CONVERSION TRACKING
Assuming you’re not running ads for the sake of branding, your goal will always be to
convert. Before spending money, you need to understand how conversions work for
Google Ads, and do the set up work to capture conversion data.
Conversion Actions is the term Google uses for any desired outcome that you get from
your advertising efforts. You will define your Conversion Actions in the Tools and
Settings, and you can set up as many as you like. Here are the basic options…
In most cases you will be using Website actions. These can include leads, purchases,
add to carts, etc. The simplest way to define a Conversion Action is to provide the URL
that a user would land on after the desired action. For example, the thank you page
after a purchase.
While you’ll want to watch your ad spend like a hawk,
remember that the ads are only half the battle. By having pages
that convert, you’ll be able to outspend you competitors.
CONVERSION ACTIONS
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 24 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
25
To make Conversion Actions work, there are two different types of “tags” you need to
install on your website. A tag is a small piece of code. While installation of these tags
is simpler than it sounds, we’ll guide you in the right direction even if you are a
complete non-techie.
The 2 types of conversion tracking tags you need to install are...
Since adding Conversion Tracking to your website(s) is essential for running ads, you’ll
want to tackle this immediately. Here are your options...
The good news is that most modern website systems, such as Wordpress or
Clickfunnels, give you the ability to add tags without touching actual code. This is
often done by copy and pasting into a box, and they usually provide instructions (or
you can find easily with a Google search).
INSTALLING TRACKING
1. Universal Site Tag
Will be installed on every page of your site (only needs installed once)
2. Event Snippet
Will be installed on just one page for each individual Conversion Action
You’ll see this screen after you set up a Conversion Action
Option #1 	Install Yourself
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

26
If you plan to install multiple Google Tags, or if you have various pixels
from other advertising platforms, you may want to use Google Tag
Manager. GTM allows you to add code once, and then manage all of
your tags from their easy to use interface (10xresource.com/ga14).
Fiverr is a marketplace with very inexpensive service providers. There
are a number of freelancers who focus specifically on installing
Google tracking code and can do it quickly and easily. You can find a
list of providers here 10xresource.com/ga15
As you may have heard, issues with privacy and Apple’s iOS devices
have caused some challenges with ad tracking. That said, the
primary means of tracking is still from conversion code installation.
If you want to improve tracking accuracy, you can also feed data to
Google via API (10xresource.com/ga16). This is more advanced and
requires a technical person.
While it is good to be able to install tracking yourself, you shouldn’t let it hold you up.
Just make sure that you have set up your Conversion Actions and then follow
Google’s instructions to provide the tag code to a professional. Just be sure to only
give access to someone you trust and/or has a good reputation.
Option #2 	Hire Someone
I waste zero time doing anything technical. Remember that
even if things cost you money, it costs you more money if
you’re not focused on marketing, sales and delivery.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 26 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
27
Study keywords, and specifically how often different terms
are searched. It is not only good for targeting, it helps you
to understand what your audience wants. Stop guessing.
KEYWORD RESEARCH
KEYWORDS AND RESEARCH
Keywords are at the heart of Google advertising. While they are most commonly used
for Search Ads, keyword targeting is also used in video, shopping, local, app and
discovery ads as well.
Simply put, a keyword is a phrase that a user types into a search. Keywords may vary in
length. Shorter terms are called “short tail” phrases, and are generally searched more
frequently. Longer terms are referred to as “long tail”, and are less targeted but more
targeted.
The Keyword Planner in the Tools section is essential for discovering keywords, and
therefore better understanding your audience. You’ll see two different options, both of
which are extremely valuable...
Short Tail example: real estate investing
Long Tail example: learn how to invest in real estate with no money down
. 	.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 27 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
28
Take some time to utilize the keyword recommendation tool (10xresource.com/ga17).
As you explore, you can also save keywords by creating ad groups. You can also export
terms and keyword data by clicking the arrow icon.
We will discuss keywords more in the Search Advertising chapter.
While I don’t like to waste a lot of time with fancy research,
it is wise to look at what your competitors are doing. Start
paying attention to what advertisers are spending big money.
You aren’t going to please everyone, and you don’t need to.
There are billions of potential buyers out there, so hone in
on the ones that are most likely to give you money.
AUDIENCE AVATARS
In addition to some basic research, you will also want to step back and think about
your customers a little deeper. Never lose track of the fact that there are real human
beings on the other end of your advertisements.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 28 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
29
A common marketing technique is to develop customer “avatars”. These are fictitious
characters that you create by defining their demographics, personality traits and
habits. Once you’ve defined avatars, you can then craft your ads for those specific
individuals.
Creating avatars can help you both in your targeting and in crafting your ad. It is much
more powerful to write an ad for a specific individual than to address an unknown
group of people.
Up until this point, we’ve talked a lot about how ads work. Now it’s time to get in the
game and get some ads up and running!
While it is important to understand the details, remember that
the best way to learn is by doing. Google makes it all step-by-step.
Get your first ad running today and watch it closely.

The set up involved with each type of ad is going to be different. We will teach you
important aspects about each ad type in upcoming chapters. For now, we’ll discuss the
most important and universal steps.
Again, the settings on the 3 levels will vary by campaign type, but it helps to understand
how each level works together.
The first question you’ll get when creating a new campaign is what your objective is. If
you sell products online, you’ll likely choose Sales. If you sell higher priced items with a
long sales cycle, you might want to go with Leads.
The next step in the process is to define your Conversion goals. This tells Google what
type of action makes your ad a success. They use this information to optimize and decide
who sees your ads. Your Conversions will then be reported to you in the Ads Manager.
QUICK REVIEW...
PRO TIP
Campaign Level - Choose ad type, goals, budget and more.
Ad Group Level - Define your keywords and/or audience.
Ad Level - Create ad including headline, description, etc.
CAMPAIGN SETUP
This step simply tells Google how to tailor your ad setup process.
If you want to be flexible and get all options, choose “create without guidance”.
For example, when setting up a video ad without guidance, you can choose the
specific type of video ads you run. This includes the ability to run sequential ads.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

32
Before you run an ad, look at it objectively and ask yourself if
you would click it yourself. Don’t be afraid to try some weird
stuff. You have to demand attention in today’s world.
While all campaign types differ, certain marketing best practices will always hold true.
Here are some tips for creating great ad headlines and descriptions…
First and foremost, always be sure to abide by Google’s ad policies (10xresource.com/ga18).
Generally speaking, if you run your ads ethically, you’ll be fine. One thing worth noting is
that Google does not allow making unsubstantiated claims.
For example, don’t say stuff like…
As long as it is substantiated, it is fine...
Which is a true statement. If you haven’t been, you don’t know what you’re missing!
AD SETUP
Grab their attention with an immediate “hook”.
It’s all about the benefit to the viewer, not about you.
Your goal is to alleviate their pain or help seek pleasure.
Your space is always limited, so make every word count.
Show how you’re better without slamming competition.
Numbers stick out, especially random numbers.
People love words like free or guarantee (if you offer).
Always make your desired “call to action” super clear.
We are the #1 best marketing conference on the planet
Forbes named 10X GrowthCon the #1 marketing conference
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

At the end of the day, your goal is to spend as much money as
you possibly can at a profit. Start small enough that it won’t
break the bank, but big enough that you can get results.
BUDGET BASICS
BIDDING STRATEGIES
BUDGETS AND GROWTH
As discussed, you will manage your ad budgets on the Campaign level. Every ad
campaign will have its own budget, which can be easily edited at any time.
While you have options, we recommend using daily budgets. This makes it much
easier to keep track of your individual campaign budget, as well as your total daily
budget across all of your ad campaigns.
Remember that Google is a big auction. While they do favor advertisers with high
Quality Scores, your bidding strategy can make a difference.
As with everything in Google Ads, every campaign type will be unique. Sometimes you
need to set specific bids, other times you don’t. With that said, you’ve got a few basic
options you’ll see when it comes to how you bid on ads.
You’ll likely start with much lower budgets than Grant!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 33 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
34
Depending on how you create your campaigns, you may get the option to set your own
bid. For example, if you choose a Sales objective, you’ll get an option to set a Target CPA
(cost per conversion action). Setting this number doesn’t guarantee you’ll get
conversions at that price, but it does help guide Google.
When launching a campaign start with the Maximum
Conversions bid strategy. Once Google optimizes for your
conversion event and you have a sense of the achievable
CPA you can expect based on your Audience and Ads you
can dial in further with Target CPA strategy.
Advanced video ad setup allows you to bid per video view. 10 cents is a good start.
Manual Bidding
If you prefer to allow Google to decide on your bids, you can choose any of their
automated “smart” strategies. Again, the options available will be determined by the
campaign type and the objective you select. Here are some of the available bidding
strategies…
Smart Bidding (Automated)
� Maximize conversions
� Maximize CPV
� Maximize ROAS
� Enhanced CPC
� Target CPM
� Viewable CPM

AUDIENCES AND TARGETING
The right message in front of the wrong audience will never hit the mark. In this
section we’ll take a look at the many ways you can target demographics and create
audience “Segments” in Google. As you’ll see, the possibilities are endless.
While all clicks are certainly not created equal, having the ideal audience will always
get more clicks and more conversions.
Remember, your audience is just as important as your ad…
Ad x Audience = Clicks
PEOPLE BASED OPTIONS
1 	DEMOGRAPHICS
These are the most common and easily adjusted demographics including age,
gender and income.
2 	AUDIENCE SEGMENTS
This is where you can start getting really detailed with your targeting. All
audience segments are based on users, their activity, and/or perceived interest.
Audience segments options are broken into 3 sections...
a. Custom Segments - These options pull in data from Google’s vast network
and find you people based on their past behavior. Options include…

when you click this option.

As you adjust your segmenting options, Google tallies your potential audience size

Finding the perfect segment for your business can be a game changer. There are

People who showed interest in purchasing
a type of product

Google search

(click to expand)
In-market segments - People who have shown an active interest in a specific interest
or area.
Life events - Targets based on what is going on in a user’s world, such as business,
love life or retirement.
Detailed demographics - More gradual details on the individual including home
ownership, education, and employment.
Affinity segments - This is a massive list of interests, from hobbies to politics to
shopping style.

Once your campaigns have run long enough, Google will suggest
Audience Segments in the “Insights” tab of each campaign that
convert best for your product or service. You can launch your
best ad/s to each of these segments for the highest likelihood of
getting conversions at the best costs.
These are ways that you can narrow your reach based on the specifics of where and how
your ads are placed.
CONTENT-BASED OPTIONS
a Keywords - This is the most important aspect of search campaigns, but can
also be used for video campaigns and other types.
b
Topics - Choose from a wide range of interests and demographics. Google will
display your ads on videos or web pages that are about that particular interest.
(Topics are no longer available for Youtube conversion campaigns. They are still usable in Cost Per View
campaign bid strategy)
c
Placements - You have the option of choosing exactly where your ad will be
displayed. This includes particular websites, apps, YouTube channels, and even
individual YouTube videos.
(Placements are no longer available for Youtube conversion campaigns (which we recommend).
They are still usable in Cost Per View campaign bid strategy. Or you can create a Custom Segment that
includes the YouTube Channels, specific videos, video lineups, URLs, Apps, or collections. We will review
this on page 40.)

Here are some of the additional features or settings you will come across when

ADDITIONAL OPTIONS
Devices - Since traffic watching
Youtube on televisions can’t
easily navigate to your website,
we recommend excluding TV.
Frequency Capping - If you are running multiple ads or spending significant money,
you may limit this to 1-2 views per week on cold traffic targeted campaigns.
Ad Schedule - Depending on what you sell, you will find certain times of day to
convert better. This includes days of the week and times of day.
Inventory Type - You can choose to exclude videos that use profanity or other mature
content.
Exclude App Placements - In
the top menu bar on the right
click “Tools and Settings”, and
under Shared Library click
“Placement Exclusion Lists”. Click
the blue plus sign on the left side,
then “New List”. Name the list
“Mobile Apps Exclusion”, and
under the Enter box type
“mobileappcategory::69500”, click Save. Click into your new exclusion list and ensure
each campaign includes the list - this will save your ad spend for non-app placements
on all campaigns.

AD STRATEGIES
Search ads are Google’s flagship program and the largest potential source of traffic in
the world. They are easy to create, but difficult to master. Here we go!
One of your first steps in creating a search ad campaign is to choose your ad extensions.
Ad extensions are the various “add-ons” that get attached to your standard search ad.
They are easy to set up and give your ads a good boost in conversion. You can also reuse
your extensions across multiple campaigns.
AD EXTENSIONS
GOOGLE SEARCH
Sitelinks - add links to various site pages
Callouts - additional text to grab attention
Call - add your business phone number
Image - add an image or company logo
Promotion - add a special discounted deal
1
2
3
4
5
Additional Extension Options:
∙ Lead Forms - collect leads in search ad
∙ App - if your app is on Android or iOS
∙ Location - show address and store hours
∙ App - direct link to download on mobile
∙ Price - show your pricing directly in ad

2
3 4

Like with everything else on Google, setting up search ads is step-by-step. They even
provide a handy feedback tool that gives you advice to make your ads better...
What’s even better, you are able to add multiple headlines and descriptions, and they
are automatically tested to find the best combination. Google calls this “responsive”
search ads, and it is a standard feature with no extra action needed.
Let’s quickly review each component of a search ad...
The most important thing here is that you copy and paste from your website and
include the https:// at the beginning (like https://grantcardone.com). You can
optionally add display path terms like /deal or /limited-time.
CREATING SEARCH ADS
Don’t stress if you don’t grade well, the real score is your sales.
A. Final URL
The headline is the most important aspect, as it is the big blue text that the user
actually clicks on. You’ve got up to 30 characters to convey your value. Always use
action oriented words and make the call to action crystal clear.
B. Headlines
A
B
C

on page 32
EXAMPLE SEARCH ADS
Score: 9 out of 10

description and use of site link ad extensions. Could have maybe added a deal ad extensions.
Score: 7 out of 10
Critique: Solid headline that makes a clear and compelling offer. They pushed their
company name hard for brand recognition. Great description, but no ad extensions.

47
Always be sure to run search ads for your company and
brand name. Even if you rank organically, you don’t want
to miss out on your most valuable potential customers.
The number of keywords you include in each ad group will depend on your overall
strategy. Generally speaking, the less traffic terms get, the more terms you’ll want to
group together. Consider giving high traffic terms their own ad group.
In addition to choosing relevant keyword phrases, you also have the capability to give
phrases one of 3 different “match types”. Here is a description of each, with the term
real estate investing as they would appear.
Do your research in the Keyword Planner in the Tools.
Broad Match
How it works: most general, Google will serve ads on any similar terms.
Example format: real estate investing (no special formatting).
1
Exact match
How it works: ad only appears on searches for that exact phrase, no variations.
Example format: [real estate investing]
3
Phrase match
How it works: more specific, generally only searches that contain the phrase.
Example format: “real estate investing” (would include real estate investing tips).
2
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 47 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
48
In addition to defining what terms you want people to find, you can also tell Google
specific terms you don’t want to come up for. Negative keywords filter out unwanted
traffic, saving you money. Here are a few examples…
Add Negative Keywords
Problem: you sell high-end services, but people keep
clicking your ads expecting something free.
Solution: add the word “free” to your negative keyword list.
Problem: you sell snacks including potato chips, but
people are coming from the term “poker chips”.
Solution: add the word “poker” to your negative keyword list.
There are billions of potential customers on YouTube. Regardless of what you sell, you
can sell more of it when you run video ads. We’re going to break down how it works and
what you need to do to get in the game.
IMPORTANT NOTE: Your video ads will actually be uploaded onto YouTube, so
you’ll need a YouTube channel to get started. You’ll likely want your ad videos to
be labeled as unlisted, and then you’ll paste the videos URLs into Google Ads
when prompted.
Before you create your first video ad, you should understand how videos turn into sales.
While there are many types of video ads, which we will discuss, they all have the same
basic steps and metrics for success...
VIDEO AD BASICS
YOUTUBE ADS
Impression 	View 	Clicks 	Conversion
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 48 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
49
We’ve found that the simpler the landing page, the higher
the conversion rate. Assume that when somebody clicks, they
want it. Don’t put extra stuff in their way to distract them.
VIDEO AD TYPES
Here are the key statistics involved in the process...
A view is considered any time a user watches a video for 30 seconds or more. This is
important, because in the case of an in-stream ad, you won’t be charged until a user
hits 30 seconds. Pay attention to your view rate to ensure your videos are grabbing
people’s attention.
After getting someone to watch, the next step is to get them to click. Your click
through rate will be affected by the effectiveness of your entire ad, including the
headlines and button. You’ve got to make your offer and solution compelling enough
to leave YouTube and go to your site.
While this stat is driven by your website or funnel, keep in mind that the more
convincing your ad is, the more likely they are to convert after clicking. That said, if
your view rate and your click through rate are strong, you likely have a website
conversion problem. Watch each step.
There are a number of options for video ads, but the first few are the ones you see
most frequently…
View Rate 	Cost Per View
Click Through Rate 	Cost Per Click
Conversion Rate 	Cost Per Conversion
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 49 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
50
Stop for a minute and recognize how truly powerful YouTube
ads are. They can not only generate immediate revenue, they
will put you and your brand in front of millions of people.
Skippable ads will be your best option for new audiences
Video ads that play before other videos and can be skipped after the viewer watches for
5 seconds. With CPV (cost per view) bidding, you are charged after a user views
30 seconds or more.
Skippable in-stream
Short 15 second video ads that play before other videos, but can’t be skipped. These ads
up CPM (cost per thousand) bidding, so you pay for impressions.
The other types of video ads are less frequently used…
Non-skippable in-stream
These are discovery ads that show up as recommended video thumbnails in the
viewers feed.
In-feed
These work exactly like non-skippable in-stream ads, but they are only 6 seconds
in length.
Bumper
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 50 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
51
Ads that are displayed access the web on various websites, initially shown without
sound.
Outstream
These are high cost ads that are ran on the homepage, available by special order only.
By far the most important part of a video ad is the messaging. Regardless of the format
or style of your video, you will never go wrong if you use this simple 3-step formula…
The first 10 seconds of your video are everything. Don’t come in flat and boring. Demand
attention by using movement and a strong first line. It’s all about calling out your specific
audience, and telling them exactly what is in it for them so they can’t turn away.
Masthead
PRO TIP
You will need to choose the “create a campaign without a goal’s guidance”
objective in order to be able to choose your video ad type. Otherwise, Google
will decide for you.
VIDEO AD SCRIPTING
Grant’s ads aren’t fancy, but they pack a strong punch.
1 	Hook the Viewer
2 	Provide Them Value
3 	Give Call to Action
1 	Hook the Viewer
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 51 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
52
VIDEO AD CREATION
Your video needs to do more than make a big promise; it’s got to back it up. You may
want to teach the user something new, or show them a demonstration of what your
product and service can do. Regardless, be sure it is focused around the result it can
provide them.
2 	Provide Them Value
3 	Give Call to Action
People need to be told EXACTLY what to do. Be crystal clear that the viewer needs to click
the button, and after they do, what their next step is. Try not to oversell, just get the click.
Don’t be afraid to give your call to action a few different times, in a few different ways.
Keep in mind, you can create video ads without filming anything. For example, there are
plenty of successful ads that use stock footage (such as you’d find on storyblocks.com)
and just add a voiceover on top. That said, the simplest and most common formula for
winning video ads is speaking directly to the consumer.
Your video ads should feel like a perfect solution aimed at a
unique individual. It is better to make several simple ads that
are targeted, than one fancy ad that speaks to no one.
5 SECRETS TO HIGH CONVERTING ADS
Identify the big problem/opportunity
Incorporate a story and/or emotion
Indirectly give yourself credibility
Inject some humor and/or personality
Immediate need to take action now
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 52 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
53
Don’t worry if you don’t have fancy equipment. Don’t worry if
you don’t want to be on camera. You could use a flip phone
with generic stock footage if the messaging is right.
Editing - Depending on the complexity of your video, you can likely edit using an
online editor such as wevideo.com. Always start by doing all the hard cuts, then
add fancy stuff.
Music - Music can help the quality and energy of the video, but just make sure it
isn’t too loud. If you want to add music, do it at the very end after the video is fully
edited.
Outsourcing - If you’d rather not mess with any of this stuff, you can always
outsource the editing. You can hire a regular editor at upwork.com or a cheap gig
at fiverr.com.
Some quick tips on producing your ads…
Want to learn more about
creating great videos?
Be sure to check out the 10X YouTube System in order to learn more about
planning, recording, editing, and uploading videos.
Video - Generally speaking, any modern smartphone will get the job done. Just
edit the settings to get the highest quality possible. Videos with movement are
always good.
Audio - Audio is the most overlooked aspect for most new video creators. The
audio needs to be on point, so consider getting an external mic to plug into your
camera.
Lighting - Make sure that the subject in your video (which might be you or
whoever is the star) is well lit, and doesn’t have shadows on their face. Outdoors
can be tough.
Some quick tips on filming your ads…
PRO TIP
To get a video ad up quickly without having to shoot and edit video footage, you can take
your best image ad, and layer an audio voiceover. So the viewer hears your voice and sees
a still image. This ad format catches attention by not having a moving video. My team has
found these fast ads to work quite well if the audio is scripted and passionate.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

OPTIMIZING AND SCALING
Managing your Google Ads is a DAILY job. You need to keep a constant eye on your
money, and be improving constantly. To oversimplify your job as an ad manager, you’ll
be doing these 4 things...
The most common adjustments you’ll make is to pause campaigns, ad groups or ads.
As a general rule, don’t delete anything unless it was a mistake.
ONGOING MANAGEMENT
Creating a Daily Routine
1 	Killing the losers 	2 	Adjusting budgets 	3 	Testing new audiences 	4 	Launching new ads
Golden Rule:
If you don’t get a conversion after spending 2X the conversion value.

55
Think of your ads as an ongoing experiment. You are in
constant search of the right messaging and the right audience.
Finding the right combination can literally make you millions.
In addition to checking your ads daily to adjust bids and pause losers, you’ll also want to
get a weekly routine. On a weekly basis, you’ll always want to make sure that you…
To dig deeper into the numbers, click the Reports icon at the top of the page. While you
can create your own custom reports and dashboard, you can get most of what you need
by using the predefined reports.
Creating a Weekly Routine
1 	Test multiple new audiences 	2 	Create and launch new ads 	3 	Deep dive into data
Scale Vertically
Increase your daily budget on winning campaigns.
Scale Horizontally
Add additional ads and ad groups.
Golden Rule:
Increase budgets by between 10%-25% per day if it is converting.
Golden Rule:
Constantly test audiences that are both more targeted and more broad.
Recommended Resource:
Management and Optimization Checklist
We’ve provided a detailed checklist to help you know what
to focus on once your ads are launched. Be sure to watch
the video overview to understand what it takes to scale
your campaigns and maximize your ROAS.

-- 55 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
56
Here are a few recommendations of what to be looking at...
▶ Gender - This is the easiest adjustment. If you’re limited in budget, and you see males
or females consistently performing better, go all in.
▶ Age - After getting some conversions, look at the ages that have converted, and
uncheck boxes for age ranges that aren’t performing.
▶ Devices - Review conversions based on device type. Depending on your landing page,
desktop or mobile may greatly outperform the other.
▶ Scheduling - Limits the windows that ads are served based on best results. For
example, only during business hours or only on weekends.
▶ Income - This one is tricky. Everyone wants to attract customers with money, so that
means higher income levels are more competitive, and therefore more expensive. High
income is a definite for high-priced offers, but if you sell inexpensive products try
targeting low income.
Don’t count people out based on their age until you’ve got
data to back it up. Old people are surprisingly tech savvy, and
young people spend way more money than you would think.
PRO TIP
Google will often use percentages to represent the amount of people to a
particular target audience or demographic. -100% means nobody will see it.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 56 of 59 --

© 2023 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
57
While optimization is important, try not to get lost in the details.
Step back from your ads and ask what people really want. There
is no substitute for matching your market and message.
▶ Location - Beyond basic countries and even cities, consider creating hyper targeted
target radiuses. For example, universities or airports.
Don’t forget that half the battle is the page you send people to. The most powerful thing
you can ever do is to have your ad messaging and your landing page completely in sync.
Be sure to check out the 10X Sales Funnel System in order to learn more about what
type of funnels to build, how to create them, and how to maximize your sales.
PRO TIP
Keep a log of lessons you learn every week. This will not only be valuable for
learning, you’ll be able to see when and why you get a change in results at
any point.
What experimentation did you try? What were the results?
What hooks/angles got lots of clicks? What ones did not?
What messaging created the best quality customers?
What videos did people stay on for? What does that tell you?
What targeting performed the best? How can you refine or expand?
Ad messaging X Landing page =
